tree.heading: Section1:CLINICALLYSUSPECTED IGAVNEPHRITIS
tree.content_preview: We recommend that a kidney biopsy is undertaken to confirm severe nephritis.

tree.heading: Section3:MANAGEMENTOFACUTEGIBLEEDING
tree.content_preview: We suggest corticosteroids within 3 days of onset of severe abdominal pain or acute Gl bleeding after exclusion of other causes including intussusception.

tree.heading: SectiOn4:MANAGEMENTOFSUSPECTEDORPROVENINTUSSUSCEPTION
tree.content_preview: We recommend that specialist surgical and [/or] radiological advice for abdominal symptoms suggestive of intussusception.

We suggest that testicular involvement (orchiditis) should be considered in boys who develop painful scrotal oedema associatedwithpalpablepurpuriclesions.

tree.content_preview:
We suggest a skin biopsy in atypical purpuric/petechial rash or to exclude alternative diagnoses.

tree.heading: SeCtiON7:DEFINITIONOFPERSISTINGORRECURRENTDISEASE
tree.content_preview:
We suggest that a typical purpuric/petechial rash persisting for more than 1 month should be defined as persisting disease.

tree.heading: Section8:LONGTERMFOLLOWUPINIGAV
tree.content_preview:
We recommend long term follow up if they have evidence of nephritis or for 3 years if they have experienced biopsy proven nephritis.

tree.content_preview: A total of 31/33 (94%) of the papers [9-23,1,24-38] mentioned an investigation to confirm nephritis and these all used the kidney biopsy to define the histological disease activity. Of those suggestin

tree.heading: Absolute indications:
tree.content_preview:
Persisting severe proteinuria (UP: UC >250 mg/mmol for 4 weeks)
Persisting moderate proteinuria (UP: UC 100-250 mg/mmol for 3 months)
Acute kidney injury stage 1 or greater (serum creatinine >1.

tree.heading: Relative indications:
tree.content_preview:
Reproducible severe proteinuria (UP: UC >250 mg/mmol) at any time point
Serum creatinine above upper limit of normal
